name: Urolithin A was identified in renal calculi from older adults
summary: A 2026 case series adds a low-confidence human safety boundary for urolithin A exposure and urinary-stone risk without quantifying incidence or proving supplement causality.
statement: A Kidney International Reports case series identified urolithin A in urinary stones from eight patients aged 59 to 87 years; among six contacted patients with exposure histories, all reported regular ellagitannin-rich foods and none reported urolithin A supplement use.
quantitative_note: The urolithin A fraction ranged from 5% to 80% of stone composition across eight reported patients.
caveats: This is a case series, not an incidence study or controlled safety trial.
Reported exposures mostly involved diet rather than direct urolithin A supplementation.
The finding does not establish that urolithin A supplements cause kidney stones, but it weakens confidence in long-term urinary-safety assumptions.
